title: Chapter 32: The Testimony
content: The fluorescent tube above Bay 14 had been flickering for six hours — a stuttering white pulse that carved the darkness into fragments. Lucy Chen had counted every one. She'd counted the screws in the ceiling panels (twelve per panel, four panels visible from her chair). She'd counted the drain holes in the floor grate (eighteen). She'd counted her own heartbeats, because counting was the only thing that kept her from screaming.
Forty-three minutes.

She could hear the guards in the corridor, their voices muffled by the reinforced glass but not erased. Night shift. The lazy ones. They'd been talking about the sedation schedule since 3:15 — 4:30 AM, regular protocol, prep the women for transport to Sublevel B before the IPO market open. Standard operating procedure for Alexander Vlad's most valuable inventory.
Eight hundred and forty-seven women. Catalogued like product.

Lucy shifted on the medical chair. The vinyl was cold against her bare thighs, sticking slightly where sweat had gathered during the night. The antiseptic smell was everywhere — isopropyl alcohol and something harsher beneath it, something metallic that she knew now was the cleaning agent they used on the IV stands. The IV stands with their needles. The needles with their teeth.
She stood. Slowly. The leg cuff clicked against the chair frame — a soft sound, but enough to make her freeze. She waited. The guards' conversation continued, undisturbed.
Three steps to the intercom panel.

She'd measured them days ago. Three steps from the medical chair to the wall-mounted unit with its cracked plastic housing and its single red transmit button. The panel was supposed to be for nurse-to-patient communication, a relic from when this place pretended to be a medical facility. But the shortwave relay behind it — that was the thing Maya had found in the schematics. A legacy emergency broadcast system, hardwired into the building's infrastructure, its frequency range reaching well beyond the Santa Cruz Mountains.
If the firewall was down, the signal would carry.
Kapoor was supposed to open the firewall at 4:00 AM. Seventy seconds. That was the window.

Lucy positioned herself in front of the intercom. Her fingers found the transmit button — smooth, cold plastic, slightly greasy from a thousand pressed palms. She pressed it experimentally. No click. No power. Not yet.
She looked to her left, through the glass partition into Bay 15.

Elena was sitting up in her chair, watching. The Romanian woman's face was pale in the fluorescent half-light, dark circles under her eyes, her hair lank against her skull. But her gaze was steady. It had been steady for three weeks — ever since Lucy had shown her the Morse code tapping, ever since they'd worked out the communication grid bay by bay through the facility.
Elena, who had a daughter in Bucharest. Elena, who hadn't spoken her daughter's name aloud in eleven days because saying it made her cry, and crying here was a kind of surrender.
Lucy looked at her.
She mouthed the words: *It's time.*
Elena nodded. Once. Definite.
The intercom panel hummed. Power.
Lucy's finger found the transmit button and pressed.
The click was louder than she expected — a sharp mechanical snap that seemed to fill the bay. She felt it in her sternum, that click. The sound of a weapon being cocked.
She opened her mouth.

For one terrible second, nothing came out. Her throat was dry, her tongue thick with the residue of fear-sweat and the bitter aftertaste of the sedative they'd given her two nights ago. The taste of survival, she thought. The taste of not being dead yet.
Then she spoke.

"My name is Lucy Chen." Her voice came out rough but clear, resonant through the intercom's tinny speaker, bouncing off the bay walls and back at her. "I am a prisoner. I am being held against my will at a facility called Site C, located in the Santa Cruz Mountains, California. I am recording this testimony on the morning of November 15th, 2026, at approximately 3:51 AM."

She paused, letting the words land. Letting them travel. Somewhere beyond these walls, beyond the redwoods and the fog, the signal was climbing through the cold mountain air, searching for a receiver.

"I was recruited through an app called Lumina. It offered personalized wellness optimization. Biofeedback. Meditation protocols. I downloaded it because I was tired. Because I was a second-year graduate student who couldn't sleep and thought maybe an algorithm could help me rest." A breath. "The retreat invitation came six weeks later. A wellness retreat in the mountains — all expenses paid. Three days of rest and restoration. That's what the email said."
The vinyl chair creaked behind her. She didn't sit. She would give this standing.

"The retreat was a processing center. The meditation rooms were intake bays. The wellness coaches were handlers. And the IV lines they hooked us to on the first night — those weren't vitamins. They weren't supplements. They were extraction lines. Blood draws. Every night. While we slept, or while we were sedated into sleeping."
She pressed the button harder, as if she could squeeze more signal from it.

"The needles have modified tips. When I say modified, I mean they're shaped like teeth. And when I say teeth, I mean teeth. This is not medical equipment. This is something else. This is designed to mimic — to replicate — something that should not exist. And it is being used on human beings. On women. On eight hundred and forty-seven women."
The number tasted like copper on her tongue. She said it again.

"Eight hundred and forty-seven. That's not an estimate. I counted. Bay by bay, floor by floor, through every shift change and every roster list I could memorize. Eight hundred and forty-seven women, each one catalogued with an alphanumeric identifier, each one assigned a blood type and an extraction schedule, each one reduced from a person to a product entry in Alexander Vlad's database."
She named him. She let the name ring.

"Alexander Vlad. Founder and CEO of Aeterna. The man who built Lumina. The man who designed Project Carmilla — a human blood harvesting operation disguised as a biotech wellness company, operating out of facilities in the Santa Cruz Mountains, with extraction infrastructure on Sublevel B that I have personally seen, personally documented, and personally survived."
The words were coming faster now, born on something deeper than breath. She could taste them — each syllable sharp and real on her tongue, the flavor of truth spoken after months of silence.
"Sublevel B. Site C. The specific coordinates—"
She recited them. Latitude. Longitude. The access road designation. The service entrance code she'd stolen from a guard's clipboard. She gave it all.

"I want to tell you about the women," Lucy said. Her voice had shifted — no longer the formal testimony cadence, but something rawer. Closer to the bone. "Because they're not numbers. I know I gave you a number. Eight hundred and forty-seven. But numbers are what they want us to be. Numbers are what Vlad sees when he looks at us. So I need you to see something else."
She thought of Elena, three feet away behind glass.

"Elena. Bay 15, Site C. She's from Romania. She has a daughter — a little girl in Bucharest who draws pictures of her mother and waits for a phone call that never comes. Elena was recruited through a European Lumina pilot program. She thought she was participating in a sleep study. She's been here four months. She has track marks on both arms, and she hasn't heard her daughter's voice since September."
Her voice caught. She pushed through.

"Priya. Nineteen years old. Engineering student from Mumbai. She thought she was receiving a research fellowship — a fully funded position at a Silicon Valley biotech firm. They flew her to San Francisco, drove her to the mountains, and strapped her to a chair within forty-eight hours of landing. She's nineteen. She codes in three languages and she can't figure out how to get out of a room with a locked door and a leg cuff. Because that's the design. That's the architecture. You don't escape a system built to make escape impossible."
The fluorescent tube flickered. Lucy blinked against the white strobe.
"Sarah."
The name cracked in her throat like a bone breaking.

"Sarah was here for three months. Bay 22. I don't know her last name — they took our names when they brought us in, assigned us identifiers instead. But I knew Sarah. She was — she liked to hum. When they drew her blood, she hummed hymns. Old ones. Her grandmother's hymns. She said the needles hurt less when she sang."
Lucy's hand was shaking on the transmit button. She held it with both hands.

"Three months in, Sarah stopped humming. Then she stopped talking. Then she stopped moving. I watched them roll her chair out of Bay 22 on a Tuesday morning. A guard said she'd been 'transferred.' That's the word they use. Transferred."
Silence. The intercom's carrier wave hissed softly, like static in a seashell.

"Sarah mattered." Lucy's voice was a blade now — no tremor, no hesitation. "Every woman here matters. We are not specimens. We are not data points. We are not line items in a quarterly report for a company that's about to go public on the New York Stock Exchange. We are people. We are human beings. And we are being farmed."
The testimony had been going for fifteen minutes. Lucy didn't know how much time she had left — the guards said 4:30, but schedules shifted. Every second was borrowed.
She released the transmit button for a moment, flexed her cramped fingers, pressed it again.
"Maya."
The name felt different in her mouth. Not a fact to be delivered — a message to be thrown across the void.

"Maya, if you can hear this — I don't know if you can. Shortwave is unreliable. The signal could be bouncing off a thousand things between here and wherever you are. But I'm speaking as if you're in this room. Because in every way that matters, you are."
She thought of Brooklyn. She imagined Maya in a dark apartment, headphones on, a frequency dial in her fingers, the whole world narrowed to a single band of static.

"I kept my promise. I never stopped counting. I never stopped fighting. Every woman I named — every number I memorized, every roster I read, every shift change I tracked — that was me keeping my promise. That was me loving you the only way I could from inside a cage."
The tears came then. She didn't wipe them. She let them fall onto the intercom housing, onto her pressing fingers.

"And I need you to know — everything you're about to do — I'm with you. Not behind you. Not waiting for you. With you. In every frequency, in every signal, in every beat of my heart that they have not yet managed to extract — I'm with you."
She paused. The carrier wave hummed. The fluorescent light flickered.
"Now burn him down."
Footsteps.
Not the distant murmur of a lazy night shift — close, deliberate footsteps on the concrete corridor. Early. Twelve minutes early.
Lucy's stomach dropped. Her finger was still on the transmit button. She had seconds — maybe less.
She pressed harder, leaned into the intercom, and spoke with everything she had left:
"My name is Lucy Chen. I am being held against my will at Site C, Santa Cruz Mountains, California. I am alive. I am fighting. And I am not alone."
She released the button.

The intercom clicked off. The hum died. The bay fell into a silence so complete she could hear her own blood moving through her veins — the blood they wanted, the blood they'd been taking, the blood that was still hers.
The footsteps stopped outside her bay.

A face appeared at the reinforced glass window — a guard, mid-thirties, face soft with the particular blandness of men who followed orders without reading them. He looked surprised. Lucy was supposed to be sedated. Lucy was supposed to be asleep in her chair, another compliant data point in the overnight roster.
She was standing, and she was not asleep, and she was looking directly at him.
His eyes widened. He keyed his radio.

Lucy sat back down on the medical chair. The vinyl was colder now, or maybe she was. She folded her hands in her lap — left over right, the way her mother had taught her to sit in church. She closed her eyes.
She had done it.
Forty-three minutes. She had turned them into a testimony. She had turned her captivity into a signal. She had turned her silence into a weapon and fired it into the sky.
Whatever happened next — the sedation, the transport, whatever punishment Vlad's people would devise for a woman who dared to speak — she had done it.
The testimony was complete.
The guard's radio crackled. "Sedation team to Bay 14. Noncompliant — repeat, noncompliant in Bay Fourteen."
But something was wrong.
The guard frowned. His radio was buzzing — not the clean static of an open channel, but a deeper, rawer interference. A frequency collision. A signal riding on top of a signal.
Then the buzzing spread.

Lucy heard it — a rising harmonic that seemed to come from everywhere at once. The ceiling speakers. The corridor PA. The guard's radio. The intercom panel behind her, dead a moment ago, now flickering back to life with a light she'd never seen it carry before.

Chloe's network. It had to be. Piggybacking on her shortwave transmission, threading through the building's PA system like a virus through a vein, reaching into the architecture of Site C itself and making it speak.
The interference swelled — a wall of harmonic noise that made the guard step back from the window, one hand over his ear, the other still holding his buzzing radio.
And inside the noise, barely audible, like a voice heard through water—
"Lucy."
Her eyes opened.
"I heard you."

Maya's voice. Coming through the PA system. Coming through the walls. Coming through the same signal Lucy had thrown into the sky minutes ago, returning to her now like a boomerang, like an echo, like a promise kept.
"We're here. Hold on."

The connection was real. She could hear it — not the polished clarity of a phone call, but the raw, uncompressed truth of a shortwave signal bounced off the atmosphere and back. The static was part of the voice. The interference was part of the message. Maya was reaching across three thousand miles of darkness, and the darkness was not stopping her.
Lucy Chen sat in Bay 14 of Site C, her hands folded, her eyes open, the taste of her own testimony still bright on her tongue.
She was smiling.
The testimony was complete. The world was about to hear it.
The True Climax begins now.
